summary: This brief post touches on two topics: Joel distinguishes between code as production, craftsmanship, and design, arguing that writing code is fundamentally a design activity where value can be added faster than cost. He uses Apple's iPod as an example of design adding value, then teases a longer essay on craftsmanship. The post also announces summer internships at Fog Creek Software.
key_points: Writing code is design, not production — a distinction Joel considers fundamental
Design is the area where you can add value faster than you add cost
Apple and the iPod are held up as examples of companies that understand design's value
Craftsmanship in software is a recognizable but distinct quality from design
money_quotes: Writing code is not production, it's not always craftsmanship (though it can be), it's design.
Design is that nebulous area where you can add value faster than you add cost.
key_insight: Writing code is fundamentally a design activity — not production — because design is where you can add value faster than you add cost.
